best_image_source: Esri World Imagery aerial photo dated 11/13/2025
roof_system: Mixed roof types: TPO, Modified bitumen, Metal

possible_roof_systems.evidence: Large smooth white/light low-slope roof fields with a membrane-like appearance; no exposed aggregate or ribbed panel profile is visible.
The bright uniform white membrane-like appearance could also be PVC, but the aerial view does not provide PVC-specific markings or close detail.
A reflective coating over a membrane remains possible because sheet seams are not clearly resolved across much of the white field.
A distinct light-gray attached rectangular section has a weathered asphaltic look that differs from the main white membrane and could be modified bitumen.

roof_age_estimate: Likely moderate to older low-slope roof segments overall; the white field appears maintained, while the gray attached section shows more weathering. Exact age cannot be determined from aerial imagery alone.

notes: Localized dark discoloration and patchy staining are visible on portions of the white roof field.
The roof has numerous rooftop penetrations and equipment curbs, increasing flashing and leak-risk complexity.
A separate small gray roof section appears more weathered than the main white field.
No clear overhanging trees touching the roof are visible in the aerial view.

observations: Visible risk factors include dark staining or inconsistent roof color, high penetration or rooftop-unit density.
Main roof field is a large bright white/light low-slope membrane-like surface with smooth appearance.
Several rooftop units, vents, curbs, and small penetrations are visible across the main roof field.
Localized dark spots and uneven discoloration are visible on the white roof, suggesting staining, repairs, or possible drainage-related wear.
A smaller attached left-lower gray rectangular section appears materially different and more weathered than the main white field.
summary: Aerial imagery dated 11/13/2025 shows a mixed commercial roof at 5101 Quebec St, Commerce City, with a dominant smooth white/light low-slope membrane-like field and smaller distinct roof areas. The main roof presents localized dark staining/discoloration and many rooftop penetrations and curbs, which increase leak potential and flashing risk. Visible risk factors include dark staining or inconsistent roof color, high penetration or rooftop-unit density.
recommendation: Have a qualified commercial roof-coating contractor perform an onsite inspection to confirm the roof system, assess seams, flashings, penetrations, and any wet-insulation indicators, and determine whether moisture testing is warranted. If the white low-slope field is serviceable and not showing widespread moisture or substrate failure, evaluate silicone roof restoration as a potential option before a more costly overlay or replacement; a qualifying silicone system may reduce tear-off waste, cost, and disruption, create a seamless monolithic white reflective cool-roof surface, withstand ponding water when properly designed and installed, improve resistance to UV exposure and hail, and qualify for a manufacturer warranty of up to 20 years. If inspection finds widespread moisture, failed insulation, incompatible substrate, or deterioration beyond restoration, evaluate overlay or replacement instead. Confirm system eligibility, required pre

roof_zones.location: main central restaurant roof and attached white low-slope sections

candidates.evidence: Large smooth white/light low-slope roof fields with membrane-like appearance; no clear aggregate texture or ribbed metal profile is visible. White membrane default is appropriate, but exact chemistry is not resolved.
Uniform light-colored smooth low-slope surface could also be PVC, but there is no PVC-specific evidence such as markings or clearer thermoplastic cues.
A reflective roof coating remains possible because the imagery does not clearly resolve sheet seams across much of the white roof area.

limitations: High glare and low aerial resolution limit separation of TPO, PVC, and coating.
Rooftop equipment, shadows, and complex roof geometry obscure some field boundaries.
Estimated area is approximate due to multiple attached subfields.

roof_zones.location: left-lower attached light-gray rectangular roof section

candidates.evidence: This section is materially different from the adjacent bright white membrane and appears light gray with a flatter, weathered asphaltic look rather than a clean white single-ply surface.
A coated asphaltic roof is plausible because the surface appears uniform and weathered, but coating-specific variation is not clearly resolved.
An aggregate-surfaced BUR cannot be excluded at this resolution, though individual aggregate is not clearly visible.

limitations: Roll laps, aggregate embedment, and substrate details are not resolved.
The zone is small and partially affected by shadow and adjacent roof glare.
Could also be an older asphaltic section adjacent to newer membrane areas.

roof_zones.location: small dark roofed canopies/appendages along edges and near the lower-left side

candidates.evidence: Several small appendages show darker rigid, planar roof surfaces with straight edges and a canopy-like appearance consistent with metal roofing at this scale.
A dark asphaltic roof system is possible where the profile is too small to resolve, though texture details are insufficient.
Some small rooftop elements are too obscured to classify confidently from the aerial view.

limitations: These are small accessory roof elements, not the primary roof field.
Fine ribs, seams, or panel orientation are not consistently resolved.
Parts of the zone may include rooftop equipment housings rather than true roof field.

overall_limitations: Reference images are withheld, so this is a Stage 1 candidate-only assessment from aerial imagery and metadata.
Material boundaries are partly obscured by glare, shadows, and rooftop obstructions.
Percentages are approximate and should be treated as visible-share estimates rather than surveyed areas.
